dbms.title: Database Management Systems (DBMS)
dbms.description: Deep-dive examples and execution flows for SQL and Schema Design.

topics.name: Normalization Step-by-Step (1NF to 3NF)
topics.explanation: Understanding how to decompose a messy table into a normalized relational schema.
topics.example:
Scenario: You have an 'Orders' table containing multiple items per row.

[Unnormalized]
Order1 | John | Apple, Banana | New York

[1NF (Atomic Values)]
Order1 | John | Apple  | New York
Order1 | John | Banana | New York

[2NF (No Partial Dependency)]
If Primary Key is (OrderID, Item), then Name and City only depend on OrderID!
Decompose:
Table 1: OrderID -> Name, City
Table 2: OrderID, Item

[3NF (No Transitive Dependency)]
If City depends on Name (John -> New York), New York doesn't directly depend on OrderID.
Decompose Table 1 further:
Table 1a: OrderID -> Name
Table 1b: Name -> City

topics.name: 1. Basic SQL (SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE)
topics.explanation: Understand how the DBMS processes queries internally.
topics.example:
SELECT * FROM Students WHERE age > 20;

Execution:
1. FROM: DBMS loads Students table.
2. WHERE: Scans row by row.
Row 1 -> age=21 -> TRUE
Row 2 -> age=19 -> FALSE
Row 3 -> age=22 -> TRUE
3. SELECT: Returns filtered rows.

UPDATE Execution: Locates row -> modifies in-place -> writes transaction log.
DELETE Execution: Scans -> checks condition -> marks as deleted.

topics.name: 2. JOINS Deep Understanding
topics.explanation: Joins execute like nested loops internally. Only matching pairs survive in INNER JOIN.
topics.example:
INNER JOIN Execution:
Take first row from Left Table. Compare with Right Table. If match -> output row. Next row, repeat.

LEFT JOIN Execution:
Start with Left Table. Match found -> append row. No match -> fill NULL.
Key Idea: Left table connects to right, preserved at all costs.

RIGHT JOIN Execution:
Start with Right Table (Reverse logic of LEFT JOIN). Match found -> append row. No match -> fill NULL.
Key Idea: Right table is always preserved.

FULL JOIN Execution:
Combine the result of LEFT JOIN and RIGHT JOIN. Fill NULLs wherever data is missing on either side.
Key Idea: Both tables are preserved; missing matches are padded with NULLs.

topics.name: 3. GROUP BY + Aggregation
topics.explanation: GROUP BY reduces rows by creating buckets. HAVING filters these aggregate buckets.
topics.example:
SELECT dept, COUNT(*) FROM Emp GROUP BY dept HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

Execution:
1. FROM: Load Emp.
2. GROUP BY: Create groups (IT -> [5000, 6000], HR -> [4000]).
3. Aggregation (COUNT): IT -> 2, HR -> 1.
4. HAVING: IT(2)>1 -> Keep, HR(1)>1 -> Remove.

topics.name: 4. Subquery Execution Flow
topics.explanation: Inner queries run before outer queries contextually filter results.
topics.example:
SELECT name FROM Students WHERE id IN (SELECT student_id FROM Marks);

Execution:
Step 1: Inner runs first -> returns list [1, 3].
Step 2: Outer checks each row. If id is in list -> keep, else discard.

topics.name: 5. Window Functions vs Group By
topics.explanation: Window functions process data over groups (windows) without collapsing rows together.
topics.example:
SELECT name, marks, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY marks DESC) FROM Marks;

Execution:
No grouping -> rows stay same.
Sort data by marks desc.
Assign rank based on order.
Key difference: GROUP BY reduces rows, WINDOW keeps rows.

os.title: Operating Systems (OS)
os.description: In-depth OS execution flows including CPU Scheduling algorithms and Gantt Charts.

topics.name: Common CPU Scheduling Terms
topics.explanation:
Arrival Time (AT): When process enters system
Burst Time (BT): CPU time required
Completion Time (CT): When process finishes
Turnaround Time (TAT): CT - AT
Waiting Time (WT): TAT - BT

topics.name: 1. FCFS (First Come First Serve)
topics.explanation: Non-preemptive (once started, cannot stop). Prone to Convoy effect (long process delays others).
topics.example:
Process | AT | BT
P1      | 0  | 4
P2      | 1  | 3
P3      | 2  | 2

Execution Order:
P1 -> P2 -> P3

Gantt Chart:
0   4   7   9
| P1 | P2 | P3 |

Calculations:
Completion Time (CT):
P1->4, P2->7, P3->9
Turnaround Time (TAT = CT - AT):
P1->4, P2->6, P3->7
Waiting Time (WT = TAT - BT):
P1->0, P2->3, P3->5

topics.name: 2. SJF (Shortest Job First)
topics.explanation: Pick process with smallest burst time. Non-preemptive. Prone to Starvation (long jobs may never run).
topics.example:
Process | AT | BT
P1      | 0  | 6
P2      | 1  | 2
P3      | 2  | 4

Execution Order:
At time 0 -> only P1 available -> run P1
At time 6 -> choose shortest among P2(2) & P3(4) -> run P2, then P3

Gantt Chart:
0   6   8   12
| P1 | P2 | P3 |

Results (CT | TAT | WT):
P1 -> 6 | 6 | 0
P2 -> 8 | 7 | 5
P3 -> 12| 10| 6

topics.name: 3. SRTF (Shortest Remaining Time First)
topics.explanation: Preemptive version of SJF. Always chooses the process with the smallest remaining burst time.
topics.example:
Process | AT | BT
P1      | 0  | 8
P2      | 1  | 4
P3      | 2  | 2

Execution:
Time 0: Only P1 -> run
Time 1: P2 arrives (4 < remaining P1=7) -> switch to P2
Time 2: P3 arrives (2 < remaining P2=3) -> switch to P3

Gantt Chart:
0 1 2 4 7 14
|P1|P2|P3|P2|P1|

topics.name: 4. ROUND ROBIN (RR)
topics.explanation: Time-sharing system where each process gets a fixed time slice (quantum). Ensures fair scheduling.
topics.example:
Time Quantum = 2

Process | AT | BT
P1      | 0  | 5
P2      | 1  | 3
P3      | 2  | 1

Execution Step-by-step:
0-2: P1 (remaining 3)
2-4: P2 (remaining 1)
4-5: P3 (finished)
5-7: P1 (remaining 1)
7-8: P2 (finished)
8-9: P1 (finished)

Gantt Chart:
0 2 4 5 7 8 9
|P1|P2|P3|P1|P2|P1|

topics.name: 5. PRIORITY SCHEDULING
topics.explanation: Process with highest priority executes first. Lower number = higher priority. Solves starvation using Aging (increase priority over time).
topics.example:
Process | Priority | BT
P1      | 2        | 4
P2      | 1        | 3
P3      | 3        | 2

Gantt Chart:
0   3   7   9
| P2 | P1 | P3 |

topics.name: 6. MLFQ (Multilevel Feedback Queue)
topics.explanation: Most advanced algorithm. Multiple queues where processes move between queues. Short jobs get priority, while long jobs move down into lower priority background queues.

oop.title: Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)
oop.description: Deep-dive examples covering advanced OOP implementations like Exception Handling.

topics.name: 1. What is Error / Exception Handling?
topics.explanation: Exception Handling = managing runtime errors so program doesn’t crash (e.g. Divide by zero, File not found). Without handling -> Program stops. With handling -> Program continues safely.

topics.name: 2. Error vs Exception & Hierarchy
topics.explanation:
Error: Serious problem (JVM level, cannot recover like OutOfMemoryError).
Exception: Runtime issue (can handle using code like ArithmeticException).

Hierarchy:
Throwable
 ├── Error
 └── Exception
      ├── Checked Exception
      └── Unchecked Exception (RuntimeException)

topics.name: 3. Checked vs Unchecked Exceptions
topics.explanation:
Checked: Checked at compile time (e.g. IOException, SQLException). Must handle using try-catch OR throws.
Unchecked: Happens at runtime (e.g. ArithmeticException, NullPointerException).

topics.name: 4. Basic try-catch Execution
topics.explanation: Core execution flow when an exception occurs.

topics.name: 5. Multiple Catch Blocks
topics.explanation: Handling different errors specifically.

topics.name: 6. FINALLY Block
topics.explanation: Always executes regardless of whether an exception occurred. Usually used to close files or release resources.

topics.name: 7. Throw vs Throws
topics.explanation:
throw: Used to manually create an exception INSIDE a method.
throws: Used to declare an exception in the method signature.

topics.name: 8. Custom Exception
topics.explanation: Creating user-defined exceptions by extending the Exception class (Very important for interviews).

topics.name: 9. Try with Resources
topics.explanation: Advanced feature to automatically close resources without needing a finally block.

cn.title: Computer Networks (CN)
cn.description: Deep-dive examples into Subnetting and Routing algorithms.

topics.name: Calculating Subnet details from CIDR
topics.explanation: Understanding how to find the Network Address, Broadcast Address, and Range of Host IPs given an IP block.
topics.example:
Given IP block: 192.168.1.10 /28

Step 1: Understand the CIDR (/28).
Total IP bits = 32. Network bits = 28. Host bits = 32 - 28 = 4 bits.

Step 2: Subnet Mask.
28 ones: 11111111.11111111.11111111.11110000 => 255.255.255.240

Step 3: Block Size (Magic Number).
256 - 240 = 16. Subnets increment by 16 (0, 16, 32, ...).

Step 4: Find the Subnet.
The IP ends in 10. The nearest multiple of 16 below 10 is 0.
Network Address = 192.168.1.0
Broadcast Address (one less than next subnet 16) = 192.168.1.15

Step 5: Usable Host IP Range.
192.168.1.1 to 192.168.1.14 (Total 14 usable hosts).

topics.name: Distance Vector vs Link State
topics.explanation: Comparative execution of routing protocols.
topics.example:
Distance Vector (e.g. RIP):
- Based on Bellman-Ford algorithm.
- A node shares its ENTIRE routing table, but ONLY with its immediate neighbors.
- Can suffer from the 'Count to Infinity' problem.

Link State (e.g. OSPF):
- Based on Dijkstra's Shortest Path algorithm.
- A node shares information about its IMMEDIATE neighbors, but broadcasts it to the ENTIRE network.
- Faster convergence, immune to Count to Infinity.
